figures.caption: Table 1: OCL performance comparison. Final Average Accuracy AFinal(↑) and Forgetting (↓) on Split-CIFAR100, Split-ImageNet-R, and Split-ImageNet-S. Best: bold, second: underline, third: italic. RwF achieves top performance on large-scale ImageNet benchmarks while ranking competitively on CIFAR100.

Table 2: Performance under few-shot data regimes (Split-ImageNet-R). Final Average Accuracy (AFinal(↑)) as the fraction of available training samples decreases from 100% to 20%. Best: bold, second: underline, third: italic. RwF maintains the highest accuracy across all evaluated data regimes.

Table 3: Effect of routing depth on Split-CIFAR100, Split-ImageNet-R and Split-ImageNet-S. Final Average Accuracy (AFinal(↑)) as the number of HopfieldPooling (HP) layers k increases. First-k inserts HP layers in the first k transformer block; Last-k inserts them in last k blocks (12 . . . 12− k + 1). Best: bold, second: underline, third: italic.
Figure 2: Performance scaling with increasing task fragmentation (Split-ImageNet-R). Final Average Accuracy (AFinal) (↑) as the number of sequential tasks t increases from 5 to 40.

Figure 1: RwF layer: routing-augmented transformer block. Given input tokens Zℓ, a Hopfield-based associative retrieval module generates input-conditioned routing prompts Pℓ via energy-based pooling over token features. The retrieved Pℓ are concatenated with the Zℓ tokens and processed by the standard Multi-Head Self-Attention (MHSA). After MHSA, only the backbone tokens Z̃ℓ are propagated to the MLP blocks and then to the next RwF transformer layer ℓ+ 1, while P̃ℓ are discarded.
